title: Pool and Slab Allocator Patterns
description: Research pool allocator (fixed-size object pools) and slab allocator (kernel-style object caching) patterns for Aria Phase 4.2 Stage 1.3, focusing on patterns NOT present in our existing SegFit segregated allocator.

scope.pool_allocator: Fixed-size object pools for high-frequency allocation/deallocation
scope.slab_allocator: Kernel-style object caching with constructor/destructor optimization
scope.allocator_interface: Common allocator trait/interface abstraction for composability

pool_allocator: What is a pool allocator vs segregated-fit (like our SegFit)?
How do pool allocators optimize for fixed-size objects?
What data structures are used (bitmap, free list, stack)?
How are pools initialized (pre-allocate N objects)?
How do pool allocators handle pool exhaustion (grow, error, fallback)?
What performance characteristics exist (ns per alloc/free)?
What are real-world use cases (network packet pools, database row pools)?
slab_allocator: What is a slab allocator (Linux kernel slub/slab)?
How does slab differ from pool allocator?
What is object caching (reuse initialized objects)?
How do slab allocators optimize constructor/destructor calls?
What is a slab, cache, and object in slab terminology?
What data structures track slabs (full, partial, empty)?
How does slab coloring reduce cache conflicts?
What performance benefits exist over malloc (avoid initialization overhead)?
How does slab integrate with kernel memory management (kmalloc, vmalloc)?
allocator_interface: What allocator trait/interface patterns exist (Rust, Zig, C++)?
What methods should an allocator interface define (alloc, free, realloc)?
How do type-safe allocator interfaces work?
What are allocator requirements for generic code?
composition_patterns: What allocator composition patterns exist (fallback, cascade)?
How do debugging allocators wrap other allocators (bounds checking, leak detection)?
How do statistics allocators track usage (bytes allocated, peak usage)?
How do thread-safe allocators wrap unsafe allocators (locks, atomics)?
What is allocator chaining (try arena, fallback to malloc)?
How do allocators delegate to backing allocators?

deliverables: Pool allocator fundamentals and architecture (300-400 lines)
Slab allocator fundamentals and kernel implementation analysis (400-500 lines)
Comparison: Pool vs Slab vs Segregated-Fit (like our SegFit)
Allocator interface/trait design patterns (Rust, Zig, C++, proposed Aria)
Performance benchmarks (pool vs slab vs malloc)
Use case recommendations (when to use pool vs slab vs arena)
Integration with Aria's type system and wild pointers
Code examples in Rust, Zig, C++, and proposed Aria syntax

existing_baseline.segfit_segregated: 56ns allocation, 15 size classes, NOT a pool allocator (general-purpose)
existing_baseline.note: SegFit is segregated-fit, NOT pool or slab. Research should focus on pool/slab-specific optimizations.
